title: Growing rosemary from cuttings
subtitle: The reliable propagation route for a Mediterranean perennial
excerpt: Rosemary (Salvia rosmarinus) takes faster and truer from cuttings than from seed. Semi-ripe cuttings in late summer root within six weeks; the new plants are ready to plant out the following spring. The cut, the medium, the wait, the planting.
sourceNotes: RHS rosemary page (https://www.rhs.org.uk/plants/15743/rosmarinus-officinalis/details) for hardiness rating, soil drainage requirement, and the semi-ripe cutting window. Mrs Earle, Pot-Pourri from a Surrey Garden (1897), Project Gutenberg, for the late-summer cuttings practice as established Victorian Surrey-garden technique. Gertrude Jekyll, Home and Garden (1900), Project Gutenberg, on Mediterranean-herb culture in a UK climate.

glossaryTerms.definition: A shoot taken from the current year's growth where the base has begun to firm up and turn woody but the tip is still soft. The optimal stage for woody-herb propagation: soft enough to root, firm enough not to rot in the rooting medium.
The point on a stem where a leaf joins. Roots form most readily from nodes, so cuttings are trimmed to a clean cut just below a node, with the lowest leaves stripped to leave the buried section bare.

content.text: Rosemary is a Mediterranean evergreen shrub that earns its place in a UK herb garden — it crops year-round, suits roast lamb and potatoes and any number of bakes, and asks for nothing beyond drainage and sun. The plant from seed is slow and unreliable; the plant from a cutting is fast, true, and free. A single mature bush gives a hundred cuttings in a good year; six of those rooted plants is enough to share with everyone you know who wants one.

This guide covers the late-summer semi-ripe cuttings route, which is the most reliable for UK conditions. Spring softwood cuttings work in a heated propagator; winter hardwood cuttings work if a polytunnel is to hand; neither matches the August-September success rate without specialist kit.
Choosing a position

Rosemary needs full sun and sharp drainage. A Mediterranean herb is more often killed by wet winter feet than by cold — a steady -5°C through a well-drained gravel bed is no problem; a single week of waterlogged clay in January will rot the plant. South or west-facing positions are best. The plant grows to about 1.5 m in good conditions; allow that space at planting.

Container culture works well — rosemary is happy in a 30-litre terracotta pot of gritty compost (50% John Innes No 2, 50% horticultural grit). Terracotta is the right material here: the porous wall lets the rootball dry out between waterings, which is what the plant wants. Plastic pots work but watering needs more attention.
Taking the cuttings
Late August through mid-September. The cue is the year's new shoots: they should be roughly 10-15 cm long, with the lower 3-4 cm beginning to firm up and turn a pale brown — these are
semi-ripe cuttings
. The upper 6-8 cm of the shoot stays soft and green. Pure soft shoots rot in the rooting medium; pure hard shoots are slow to root. The semi-ripe stage is the window.

Take cuttings in the morning when the plant is full of moisture. Use sharp secateurs or a sharp knife — a clean cut heals faster than a torn one. Snip 10 cm lengths from the current year's growth, cutting just above a leaf
node
on the parent plant.

Drop the cuttings into a polythene bag with a few drops of water as you go, so they don't wilt on the bench. Take 50% more than you think you need — 70% strike rate is normal, and a few cuttings will rot regardless of conditions.
Preparing each cutting

On the bench, trim each cutting to length: a clean cut just below the lowest node, giving a 7-10 cm cutting with at least three nodes total. Strip the leaves off the bottom 3-4 cm — pull them off in a downward motion. A bare buried section roots cleanly; buried leaves rot and infect the cutting.

Rosemary roots without hormone powder, but a light dip in rooting hormone (a powder from a garden centre) speeds the strike rate and improves consistency. Tap any excess powder off before planting — caked hormone burns the stem.
Rooting medium and conditions

A 50/50 mix of peat-free seed compost and horticultural grit, in 9 cm pots. The mix should drain freely — water poured on top should run through the pot in seconds, not pool on the surface. Insert each cutting around 4 cm deep, firmly enough that it stays upright. Six cuttings around the edge of a 13 cm pot is the traditional arrangement; one cutting per 9 cm pot uses more pots but isolates failures.

Water once after planting, then leave the pots somewhere bright but not in direct hot sun — a north-facing cold frame is ideal, an east-facing windowsill is the indoor alternative. A clear plastic bag tented over the pot (held off the cuttings by a few sticks) holds humidity for the first fortnight; vent the bag for a few hours each day to stop fungal build-up.
Through autumn and winter, water sparingly — once a fortnight is enough. The medium should be just-damp, never wet. Most rosemary cutting failures are over-watering.
Through the season

Six weeks after taking the cuttings (mid-October from a late-August take), give the cuttings a gentle tug. A rooted cutting resists; an unrooted cutting lifts cleanly out of the medium. Don't disturb the rooted ones — just confirm they've taken.

Overwinter the rooted cuttings in their pots — a cold frame, an unheated greenhouse, or against a sunny wall protected from the worst of the rain. Don't bring them into a heated house; the warmth pushes soft growth that the plant doesn't have the roots to support, and it sets the plant back when it goes outside in spring.

March or April: pot on the rooted cuttings into individual 13 cm pots of the same gritty compost mix. Grow on through the summer; the plants are ready to plant out into final positions in late summer of the same year, a full year after the cuttings were taken.
Harvest

Once a plant is established (year two onwards), rosemary crops year-round in the UK. Snip 5-10 cm tips as needed; the plant responds with side shoots, which strengthens the bush. Don't strip more than a third of the bush at one time, and don't cut into old hard wood — rosemary regenerates poorly from the woody base.

Flowers appear in late spring and through summer — pale blue trumpet shapes that bees work over enthusiastically. The flowers are edible (the petals are mild and slightly sweet) and the bush carries on cropping its leaves through the flowering period.
What can go wrong

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: Three common failures in rosemary propagation and what to do.

items.symptom: Cuttings rotting at the base within two weeks
items.cause: Wet medium, buried leaves, or insufficient drainage in the pot.
items.fix: Strip the bottom leaves cleanly. Add more grit to the medium (60/40 grit-to-compost on heavy clay-bottom pots). Water once after planting and not again for two weeks unless the medium is genuinely dry.

items.symptom: Cuttings wilting on the bench before they're planted
items.cause: Too long between taking and planting, or warm dry conditions in transit.
items.fix: Work in batches of ten — take, prepare, plant, then go back for the next ten. Drop fresh cuttings into a bag with a damp paper towel if there's any delay.

items.symptom: Mature plant going woody and bare in the centre
items.cause: Years of harvesting from the same side, or the plant reaching the end of its productive life (4-6 years for rosemary in UK conditions).
items.fix: Rotate harvesting around the bush. Plant a fresh cutting-propagated bush alongside an ageing one each year so there's always a productive young plant ready to take over. Don't try to hard-prune a leggy rosemary — it rarely regenerates from old wood.

content.text: Variety pointers

Common rosemary (the standard Salvia rosmarinus) is the upright bush most kitchen gardens grow; Miss Jessopp's Upright is the same shape but with a more disciplined column form. Prostratus is the trailing variety for the top of a wall or the edge of a container — less hardy in cold UK winters but striking in a pot. Avoid named modern proprietary varieties on a first attempt; the species is the workhorse and is genuinely the obvious starting point.
